fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that catalog runtime thing we talked about last sprint. Basically devs should be able to register their resources and tools once and have clients discover and call them without writing all the boilerplate themselves. Think of it like that registry pattern we used in the notification service — same idea but for resources and callable operations.

Right now without this, every team is hand-rolling their own listing logic, pagination, URI matching, and error handling, and it's all inconsistent. Clients can't reliably figure out what's available or how to call it.

The listing endpoints need to support paging — we had some complaints from the mobile team about large catalogs being unwieldy. Also the URI matching for the dynamic ones needs to pull out path variables and use them to render content, similar to how the old routing layer worked.

For the callable operations side, inputs need to have machine-readable type info attached — nested objects and arrays included — and we should be validating required fields before anything gets invoked. If something's wrong with a registration or a call, we want a clean normalized error shape, not a stack trace.

One thing I keep forgetting to mention: the cursor for paging should work the way we agreed with the frontend team. And make sure the MIME type defaulting follows the same rule we established for static content. Reach out if unclear.

trigger_keywords: default MIME type
what mime type is used when none is provided
fallback content type for resources
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When no MIME type is explicitly provided for a static resource or resource template, the system must default to 'text/plain'. This applies to both listing and reading responses and must appear in the mimeType field of every resource entry.

trigger_keywords: how does the cursor work
what format is the pagination cursor
what value does nextCursor hold
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The cursor is a decimal string representing the integer offset (index) into the registration-ordered list. For example, if page_size is 5 and you're on the first page, nextCursor is '5'. It is only emitted when there are more items beyond the current page. If the full remaining set fits in the page or no page_size is given, nextCursor must NOT appear in the response.

trigger_keywords: resource not found error shape
what does the error look like when URI is missing
unknown URI response format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a read request targets a URI that matches neither a static resource nor a template, the response must be a JSON object with exactly two fields: 'error' set to the string 'resource_not_found', and 'uri' set to the requested URI string. No other fields should appear.

trigger_keywords: template URI variable extraction
how do path parameters get substituted
render_template_values behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For resource templates, the uri_template uses {variable_name} placeholders. When a concrete URI is read, the runtime matches it against registered templates, extracts the path variable values by position, then substitutes them into the text_format string (which uses the same {variable_name} syntax) to produce the rendered text content. The response uses the concrete URI, not the template URI.

trigger_keywords: invalid resource definition error
what happens when URI is null or empty
resource registration validation failure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If a static resource is registered with a null or empty string URI, the catalog build must fail immediately and return a JSON error object with a single field: 'error' set to 'invalid_resource_uri'. Similarly, a null tool name must return 'error': 'invalid_tool_name'. These validations fire when the catalog is built, not at list/read/call time.

trigger_keywords: nested object input schema
how are object type arguments represented
inputSchema for object properties
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For a tool argument of type 'object', the inputSchema must include a 'properties' map for its sub-fields, a 'required' array listing the names of required sub-fields only (omitting optional ones), and a 'description' field. The parent-level 'required' array of the top-level schema lists only required top-level arguments. Optional sub-fields appear in properties but not in required.

trigger_keywords: array tool input schema
how are array arguments described
items field in inputSchema
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For a tool argument of type 'array', the inputSchema property entry must include 'type': 'array', a 'description', and an 'items' field. If items is a primitive type (e.g. 'integer'), items is rendered as {'type': 'integer'}. If items is 'object', the items field becomes an object schema with its own 'properties' and 'required' arrays describing the nested object structure.

trigger_keywords: tool listing response shape
what does resource_templates list return
top-level key for template listing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The list response for tools uses the top-level key 'tools'. The list response for static resources uses 'resources'. The list response for resource templates uses 'resourceTemplates' (camelCase). Each template entry includes 'uriTemplate' (camelCase), 'name', 'description', and 'mimeType'. These key names are exact and case-sensitive.

trigger_keywords: tool call response envelope
what does a successful tool call return
isError field in tool response
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A successful tool call response must be a JSON object with two top-level fields: 'content', which is an array of content items each having 'type' and 'text' fields, and 'isError' set to the boolean false. On a validation failure (e.g. missing required argument), the response must use 'isError': true and a content item describing the error. The key is always 'isError' (camelCase).

trigger_keywords: missing required tool argument error
what happens when a required field is absent
validation error for tool call
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If a tool call is missing a required top-level argument, the response must set 'isError': true and include a content item of type 'text' whose text identifies the missing argument. If a required nested object field is missing, similarly 'isError': true with a text content item naming the missing field. The outer envelope always uses the content/isError shape, never the bare error object shape used for resource errors.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the cursor should work the way we agreed with the frontend team
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The cursor is a decimal string encoding the integer start index into the registration-ordered list. nextCursor equals the start index of the next page and is only included when more items remain beyond the current page. See feature2_static_resource_pagination.json and feature8_tool_pagination.json for exact examples.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same rule we established for static content regarding MIME type defaulting
context_pointers.actual_meaning: When mimeType is absent from a resource or resource template definition, it defaults to 'text/plain'. This is specified in feature1_static_resource_listing.json and feature6_template_resource_listing.json test cases.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that registry pattern we used in the notification service
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The CatalogRuntime registration pattern: resources, resource_templates, and tools are each registered as lists during catalog build (setup phase). The build validates definitions eagerly and raises normalized errors. The runtime then serves list/read/call operations against the registered entries.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how the old routing layer worked
context_pointers.actual_meaning: URI template matching in feature5_template_resource_reading.json: the uri_template pattern uses {param_name} placeholders, matched positionally against a concrete URI path. Extracted values are substituted into text_format using the same {param_name} syntax to produce rendered output.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented. You'll want to look at the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact expected input/output shapes, or check start.md for the feature-level behavior descriptions. If it's about error formats or field naming, those are likely pinned in the relevant feature's test JSON.

feature10_nested_object_tool_inputs.json.description: A tool input may be a required object with its own required fields and optional fields.

feature11_array_tool_inputs.json.description: A tool input may be an array of primitive values and validates each element by index.

feature12_array_of_object_tool_inputs.js.description: A tool input may be an array of objects whose elements validate nested required fields.

feature13_tool_definition_validation.jso.description: Invalid tool definitions are rejected with normalized error categories.

feature1_static_resource_listing.json.description: Registered static resources are listed with their URI, display name, description, and default MIME type.

feature2_static_resource_pagination.json.description: Static resources preserve registration order and support cursor-based pagination.

feature3_static_resource_reading.json.description: Reading a registered static resource returns the requested URI, MIME type, and text content; unknown URIs are reported neutrally.

feature4_static_resource_definition_vali.description: Invalid static resource definitions are rejected with normalized error categories.

feature5_template_resource_reading.json.description: Template resources match concrete URIs, extract path variables, and render content from those variables.

feature6_template_resource_listing.json.description: Registered resource templates are listed with template URI, display name, description, and MIME type, and support pagination.

feature7_simple_tool_schema_and_call.jso.description: A tool with one required text input exposes a JSON-compatible input schema and returns text content when called.

feature8_tool_pagination.json.description: Tool listings preserve registration order and expose next cursors when a page has more entries.

feature9_multi_argument_tool_calls.json.description: Tools may declare multiple required and optional inputs; missing required inputs return normalized validation errors.
